coredata.dc:title: A highly selective fluorescent sensor for manganese(II) ion detection based on N,S-doped carbon dots triggered by manganese oxide
coredata.dc:creator: Ngeontae, Wittaya
Chaiendoo, Kanokwan
Ngamdee, Kessarin
Ruangchai, Sukhum
Saiyasombat, Chatree
Busayaporn, Wutthikrai
Ittisanronnachai, Somlak
Promarak, Vinich
coredata.dc:description: A highly selective, carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent sensor for manganese(II) ions (Mn2+) was proposed. The synthesis of N,S-doped CDs (N,S-CDs) involved simple pyrolysis of a mixture of lactose and l -cysteine in a hot NaOH solution. The as-synthesized codoped CDs exhibited a weak fluorescent emission peak at 510 nm when excited at 430 nm. In the presence of Mn2+, the intensity of the fluorescence signals was effectively increased with a small redshift in the spectrum. Thus, by tracing the degree of fluorescence enhancement, the material could be used as a selective probe for Mn2+ detection. The possible sensing mechanism was based on the oxidation of the N,S-CDs by MnO2, which was generated from mixing Mn2+ with NaOH in the N,S-CDs-containing medium. A linear relationship was established between the extent of the increase in fluorescence and the increasing Mn2+ concentration from 0 to 5.0 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.12 μM. Remarkably, even in the presence of other cations, the developed sensor remained highly selective toward Mn2+. The feasibility of our developed sensor in real applications was confirmed with environmental samples, showing excellent accuracy and precision.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemical sensors
Fluorescent sensors
Optical nanomaterials
Nanoparticles
Fluorescence enhancement
Heavy metal ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of the Mn2+ content in real water samples
Parameters affecting detection sensitivity and their optimization
Types of buffers and solution pH
Effect of the N,S-CDs volume on the Mn2+ detection sensitivity
Analytical performance of the proposed sensor
Analysis of Mn2+ concentrations in real water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT) contract number NRCT5-RSA63003-04 . WN thanks the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. This work was carried out under the Network on Emerging Sensor Designs and Fabrication Strategies, the Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI) (IRN62W0002).

This work was financially supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT) contract number NRCT5-RSA63003-04. WN thanks the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. This work was carried out under the Network on Emerging Sensor Designs and Fabrication Strategies, the Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI) (IRN62W0002).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized N,S-CDs. (b) The emission spectrum of the N,S-CDs recorded at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 nm to 520 nm. HR-TEM images of the (c) N,S-CDs, (e) N,S-CDs + Mn2+, and (d) and (f) enlarged HR-TEM images of the N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs + Mn2+, respectively.
High-resolution XPS spectra of N,S-CDs: (a) C 1s, (b) O 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) S 2p spectra.

Fluorescence spectra of (a) N,S-CDs, (b) CDs, and (c) CDs + l -cysteine in the presence and absence of Mn2+ and the absorption spectra of (d) N,S-CDs, (e) CDs, and (f) CDs + l -cysteine in the presence and absence of 10 μM Mn2+. All of the solution pH values were controlled by acetic-acetate buffer at pH 5.0.

(a) Comparisons of the normalized Mn K-edge XANES spectra of the MnO2 solid with that of Mn2+ in NaOH solution; (b) normalized Mn K-edge XANES spectra of 100 μL of N,S-CDs solution after the addition of 100 ppm Mn2+ with that of the reference materials; and (c) normalized Mn K-edge XANES spectra of the N,S-CDs solution after the addition of 100 ppm Mn2+ with that of 500 μL of N,S-CDs solution after the addition of 100 ppm Mn2+.

Fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of 5 μM Mn2+ recorded in 0.1 M (a) ACBF, (b) PBS, and (c) Tris-HCl buffer at different solution pH values. The corresponding degrees of fluorescence enhancement in (d) ACBF, (e) PBS, and (f) Tris-HCl buffer.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of 5 μM Mn2+ measured in 0.1 M ACBF, pH 5.0, at different incubation times ranging from 0 to 30 min; (b) their corresponding fluorescence intensities as a function of the incubation time (c) The fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs with and without 5 μM Mn2+ measured in 0.1 M ACBF, pH 5.0, at different incubation times ranging from 0 to 60 min after adjusting the solution pH. (d) The corresponding fluorescence intensities as a function of the incubation time.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of different volumes of N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of 5 μM Mn2+ measured in 0.1 M ACBF, pH 5.0. (b) The corresponding fluorescence intensity as a function of the N,S-CDs volume.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs in the presence of various metal ions (5 μM) measured in 0.1 M ACBF, pH 5.0; (b) the corresponding degrees of fluorescence enhancement.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs in the presence of varying concentrations of Mn2+ measured in 0.1 M ACBF, pH 5.0. (b) Corresponding calibration plot using the fluorescence intensity at 510 nm.
Determination of Mn2+ concentrations in water samples using the proposed sensor and ICP–OES technique (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A highly selective fluorescent sensor for manganese(II) ion detection based on N,S-doped carbon dots triggered by manganese oxide

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly selective, carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent sensor for manganese(II) ions (Mn2+) was proposed. The synthesis of N,S-doped CDs (N,S-CDs) involved simple pyrolysis of a mixture of lactose and l -cysteine in a hot NaOH solution. The as-synthesized codoped CDs exhibited a weak fluorescent emission peak at 510 nm when excited at 430 nm. In the presence of Mn2+, the intensity of the fluorescence signals was effectively increased with a small redshift in the spectrum. Thus, by tracing the degree of fluorescence enhancement, the material could be used as a selective probe for Mn2+ detection. The possible sensing mechanism was based on the oxidation of the N,S-CDs by MnO2, which was generated from mixing Mn2+ with NaOH in the N,S-CDs-containing medium. A linear relationship was established between the extent of the increase in fluorescence and the increasing Mn2+ concentration from 0 to 5.0 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.12 μM. Remarkably, even in the presence of other cations, the developed sensor remained highly selective toward Mn2+. The feasibility of our developed sensor in real applications was confirmed with environmental samples, showing excellent accuracy and precision.

ce:section.ce:para: Manganese (Mn) is a necessary micronutrient for the human body [1,2] and serves as a crucial component in various enzymatic metabolic processes [1], such as lipid and carbohydrate metabolism, metabolism of organic acids, growth retardation, skeletal abnormalities in neonates [3], and formation of bone and other tissues [4]. However, prolonged exposure to Mn2+ is harmful to human health because it affects the nervous and respiratory systems [5], causing adverse neurological consequences [6]. Contamination of drinking water with Mn2+ at levels higher than 14 ppm may induce manganism symptoms [6], including violent acts, hallucinations [7], mood swings, depression, and Parkinson-like syndrome [7,8]. The development of a sensitive and selective method for detecting manganese levels is therefore very important for human health and environmental protection. Among the existing valence states of manganese in natural water, Mn2+ is the most stable form [8]; thus, most efforts have been devoted to the detection of manganese in this form than in other forms.

With great advances in nanotechnology, several types of nanomaterials possessing special optical properties have been utilized in the detection research field. Carbon dots (CDs) are an interesting class of nanomaterials frequently used to fabricate fluorescent sensors because of their excellent solubility in water, low toxicity, low cost, ease of synthesis, stable photoluminescence, good biocompatibility [9], tunable photoluminescence, and multicolor emission associated with excitation [10]. These unique properties enable CDs to be applied in biomedicine and optoelectronics, particularly in sensing approaches [11,12].

In general, the basic structure of CDs consists of two major parts: a sphere-shaped core composed of stacked fragments of graphene that are arranged in an ordered or disordered manner and a surface with abundant functional groups [13,14]. Despite their excellent properties, the emission efficiency of CDs is not very high [15]; however, doping their base structures with nonmetallic heteroatoms (such as N, O, P, S, and B) results in an increase in their quantum yield [16] and changes their intrinsic properties [17,18]. Doping only N atoms into CDs improves the functional groups of CDs and increases selectivity for target species, but the fluorescent emission is not significantly improved. However, most N-doped CDs (N-CDs) possess a higher quantum yield than nondoped CDs because the nitrogenous groups on the surface change the surface state of CDs [19]. Codoping of N and S atoms into the base structure of CDs (N,S-CDs) significantly increases the fluorescent emission efficiency because doping generates an alternating surface state that facilitates a high yield of radiative recombination [19]. A carbon source along with N- and S-containing compounds is subjected to a hydrothermal treatment at high temperatures to synthesize the doped CDs. The resulting N,S-CDs have been utilized as a probe, sensing fluorescence emitted from various target analytes, particularly oxidizing compounds. For example, Ding et al. used a one-step hydrothermal method to synthesize S-CDs and N,S-CDs, whose C and S dopants were sourced from α-lipoic acid and N dopant was sourced from ethylenediamine, and then compared their fluorescent emissions. The authors reported that the N,S-CDs showed stronger fluorescent emission than the S-CDs due to the formation of CN bonds. As an Fe3+ sensor, the codoped CDs displayed fluorescence quenching in the presence of Fe3+ [16]. Several other research groups have also used N,S-CDs as sensors for a diverse range of analytes. Wang et al. used citric acid and thiosemicarbazide (an N and S source) to prepare N,S-CDs in a hydrothermal reaction. The synthesis represented a versatile avenue for detecting Cr(VI). When the system contained Cr(VI), the fluorescence signals of the codoped CDs decreased, which were subsequently recovered by the addition of Ba2+ [20]. Using a one-pot facile hydrothermal method, Cui et al. prepared N,S-CDs from acrylic acid and methionine. The proposed codoped CDs were applied as a fluorescent temperature sensor and Fe3+ probe using a fluorescent quenching strategy [21]. Chen et al. used a hydrothermal method and garlic to obtain N,S-CDs, which were then shown to comprise pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and thiophene-S with high fluorescent quantum yields. The prepared material served as a sensor for Fe3+, whose presence caused a decrease in the fluorescence intensity [19]. Wang et al. described an N,S-CDs-based uric acid sensor prepared from citric acid and thiourea using a hydrothermal method. They reported a decrease in the fluorescent emission of the codoped CDs induced by the product of the Fenton reaction (H2O2+Fe2+). Therefore, the level of H2O2, which is produced from an enzymatic reaction of uric acid, was measured and subsequently used to determine the amount of uric acid [15]. As reported by Wang et al., wool and pig hair serve as precursors for the production of N,S-CDs in a hydrothermal treatment reaction. The codoped CDs were successfully applied as a fluorescent probe for sensing Cr(VI) using the fluorescent quenching mechanism. They reported that fluorescent quenching was due to the oxidation of oxygen-containing groups and S-related species on the surface of N,S-CDs by Cr(VI) [22]. Despite their applications in detecting a diverse range of analytes, no studies have yet reported the use of N,S-CDs for the detection of Mn2+ or even MnO2 via a fluorescence enhancement strategy.

In this study, a selective and sensitive assay for detecting Mn2+ using N,S-CDs was developed. The codoped CDs were prepared through the pyrolysis of lactose and l -cysteine in a hot alkaline solution. In the presence of Mn2+ in a strong alkaline medium (MnO2), the prepared codoped CDs exhibited a stronger fluorescence intensity because the N,S-CDs were oxidized. Several characterization techniques were employed to elucidate the sensing mechanism. Furthermore, parameters affecting the fluorescence enhancement were thoroughly studied and optimized. Selectivity over similarly structured compounds and applicability of the developed assay in real water samples compared to the standard inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP–OES) technique were demonstrated. The proposed assay provided excellent benefits for the detection of Mn2+, including a short analysis time, low cost, ease of synthesis and excellent selectivity over other heavy metal ions.

All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Lactose and glacial acetic acid were obtained from QReC. Ferric (III) nitrate nonahydrate, lead (II) nitrate, nickel (II) nitrate hexahydrate and zinc nitrate hexahydrate were purchased from Fluka. Manganese (II) acetate, sodium acetate and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Carlo Erba Reagents. l -Cysteine was obtained from Sigma–Aldrich. All solutions were prepared using deionized water with a specific resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm from RiOs™ Type I Simplicity 185 (Millipore water).

Fluorescence measurements were recorded with an RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu). A slit width of 5 nm was used for both excitation and emission. The absorption of the N,S-CDs was measured with an Agilent HP spectrophotometer. Bright-field high-resolution transmission electron micrographs (HRTEM) were recorded using a JEOL JEM ARM200F transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV. Fluorescent lifetime experiments were performed using a fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Edinburgh Analytical Instruments, FS5). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) absorption spectra were measured using a Bruker TENSOR27 FTIR spectrometer. The solution pH values were measured with a UB-10 Ultra Basic pH meter (Denver Instrument). The concentration of Mn in water samples was analyzed with ICP–OES (Optima 2100 DV, USA) for comparison with the proposed method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on JEOL JPS-9010MC with a Mg Kα source (1253.6 eV) Mg at 12 kV and 25 mA.

N,S-CDs were synthesized using a previously published procedure [23] with modifications. Briefly, 0.4000 g of lactose and 0.1000 g of l -cysteine were mixed together in 8 mL of 4 M NaOH in a beaker and stirred until the solution was well mixed. Then, the mixture was heated at 90 °C on a hotplate for 5 min. The heated solution was left at room temperature to cool, followed by the addition of 8 mL of deionized water. The color of the mixture turned from colorless to light yellow and then to brown. For comparison, the undoped counterpart was synthesized in parallel using the same procedure as the N,S-CDs, except the solution lacked l -cysteine.

An appropriate volume of the 0.1 mM Mn2+ standard solution was added to a 10 mL volumetric flask containing 100 μL of the as-prepared N,S-CDs solution (containing 2 M NaOH) to achieve the desired concentrations. Then, the mixture was incubated at room temperature for 5 min, after which it was brought to a final volume of 10.00 mL using 0.1 M acetic acetate buffer (ACBF) at pH 5.0. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded with an excitation wavelength (λ ex) of 430 nm, and for quantitative analysis, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 510 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the prepared CDs was measured before and after adding Mn2+ and was assigned as F0 and F, respectively. A calibration curve was constructed by plotting the degree of fluorescence enhancement (F–F0) against the Mn2+ concentration. The proposed sensor was evaluated for its selectivity for Mn2+ over other metal ions using the same procedure except that a 0.1 mM standard solution of each possible interfering compound was added to a final concentration of 5 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the Mn2+ content in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the proposed sensor for Mn2+ detection in real water samples was determined using two different water samples. Wastewater samples were collected in HDPE bottles from rivers in Khon Kaen Province, while tap water was collected from the laboratory. Both water samples were used without any preservation. Water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μM filter membrane to separate suspended particulate matter before analysis. The water samples were added to a 10 mL volumetric flask containing 100 μL of the as-prepared N,S-CDs solution. In addition, water samples were spiked with standard solutions containing different concentrations of Mn2+ to evaluate the accuracy of the sensor. Then, the mixture was incubated at room temperature for 5 min prior to adjustment of the final volume to 10.00 mL with ACBF at pH 5.0. The fluorescence intensities at 510 nm were measured using a λ ex of 430 nm. The concentration of Mn2+ in both types of samples was calculated using the constructed calibration curve.

High-quality N,S-CDs were prepared from carbon, nitrogen and sulfur sources under high pressure and temperature conditions using the hydrothermal treatment process. The N,S-CDs obtained from this process always exhibit a high fluorescent quantum yield due to the formation of a graphitic carbon structure. In addition, pyrolysis is another method for preparing N,S-CDs. Pyrolysis does not require a special device and utilizes a relatively low reaction temperature and atmospheric pressure. Therefore, N,S-CDs are easier to prepare with pyrolysis than with the hydrothermal method. For the pyrolysis method, the reaction generally requires a highly acidic or basic medium to assist with the formation of N,S-CDs [24]. However, the quality of the obtained N,S-CDs may not be perfect compared with that of N,S-CDs obtained from hydrothermal treatment. Giannelis and coworkers [25] investigated the mechanism by which carbogenic nanoparticles are formed as a result of subjecting citric acid and ethanolamine to pyrolytic treatment. Pyrolysis at low temperatures leads to the formation of organic fluorophores. In addition, the carbogenic core is formed at a high temperature, and the mixture of both products is generated at an intermediate temperature. The authors concluded that the fluorescence of the product of the pyrolysis reaction depends on the pyrolysis temperature. Therefore, we hypothesized that if pyrolysis occurs at intermediate temperatures, a mixture of both organic fluorophores and carbogenic cores (a graphitic carbon structure mixed with amorphous carbon structures) would be obtained. The presence of an appropriate oxidizing agent would further react with the incomplete structure (amorphous carbon structures) and provide an efficient fluorophore (doped graphitic carbon structure), resulting in an increase in the fluorescent emission of the as-prepared N,S-CDs.

Synthesis of the material involved the simple pyrolysis of a mixture of lactose (carbon source) and l -cysteine (N,S source) in a strong alkaline solution via a one-pot reaction. The obtained N,S-CDs were dispersed well in water without any precipitation. Fig. 1 (a) shows the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs. A wide absorption band was observed in the 250–500 nm range with a characteristic absorption peak at 298 nm and a 330-nm shoulder absorption peak, whose occurrence resulted from a π-π* or n-π* transition of the carbon nanomaterial [26]. This characteristic of the N,S-CDs is consistent with that of intercalary semiconductor nanomaterials [27]. When the solution of the N,S-CDs was irradiated at 430 nm, an intense emission peak appeared at 510 nm. The excitation spectrum exhibited a mirror image of the emission spectrum, which is characteristic of carbon-based fluorophore materials. As shown in Fig. 1(b), the emission peak positions of the N,S-CDs redshifted from 460 to 520 nm, accompanied by a gradually decreasing fluorescence intensity when the excitation wavelengths were gradually increased from 340 to 520 nm. This phenomenon indicated that the fluorescence emission of the N,S-CDs displayed stimulus dependency. This dependence was attributed to the uneven distribution of the particle size of the N,S-CDs on the surface, leading to the formation of different emission sites [27]. The particle size and morphological characteristics of the N,S-CDs were investigated with HR-TEM. Fig. 1 (c) and 1(e) show TEM micrographs of the N,S-CDs in the presence and absence of Mn2+, respectively. Fig. 1 (c) shows that the N,S-CDs particles were spherically shaped without particle aggregation, having a size range of 3–5 nm and an average size of 3.8 ± 0.5 nm. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 1(d), the N,S-CDs had lattice fringes with interplanar distances of approximately 0.23 nm, suggesting that the synthesized N,S-CDs possessed a crystal phase in the graphitic carbon structure.

The structure of the N,S-CDs was further characterized using Raman spectroscopy, and the Raman spectrum of the N,S-CDs is shown in Fig. S1(a) (ESM). Noticeable characteristic peaks were observed with a relatively weak G band peak appearing at 1526 cm−1, which belongs to the planar graphite sp3 carbon, indicating that the codoped CDs mostly contained sp3-hybridized carbons. A relatively strong N,S-CDs band peak at 1345 cm−1 indicated a disordered crystalline structure of the sp2 cluster. Additionally, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the N,S-CDs presented in Fig. S1(b) (ESM) was evaluated to investigate their crystallinity. The XRD diffraction pattern showed a broad peak whose center was located at 2θ = 22.8°, according to the (002) lattice spacing of a graphitic-like structure.

The surface composition and chemical state of the N,S-CDs were studied with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. S2 (ESM) shows the full-scan XPS spectrum of the codoped CDs. The peaks at 169, 285, 401 and 532 eV correspond to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s respectively [28]. This result confirmed that the carbon-based structure was successfully doped with nitrogen and sulfur atoms. In addition, the high-resolution XPS spectra of each element are displayed in Fig. 2 . Deconvolution of the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2(a)) yielded four peaks at 285.087, 286.721, 288.069 and 289.479 eV corresponding to the C–C/CC, C–OH/C=N/C-SO3-C, C=O and COOH respectively. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2(b)) was deconvoluted into peaks at 532.323, 533.325 and 534.271 eV that were attributed to the COOH/C-SO3-C, C-OH and COOH bonds, respectively. In the high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2(c)), three peaks appear at 400.572, 401.552 and 402.488 eV, representing pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively. Furthermore, the high-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2(d)) clearly shows peaks at 170.280 and 169.186 eV representing S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of sulfonate (-C-SO3-C) [29]. The characterization data obtained using various techniques strongly confirmed that the successfully synthesized N,S-CDs possessed a fluorescence property suitable for use as a probe to detect fluorescence in the presence of specific analytes.

Furthermore, the fluorescent lifetime of the as-prepared codoped CDs was studied and compared with that of the undoped CDs. According to the results (Fig. S3 of the ESM), the fluorescent relaxation pattern of the N,S-CDs clearly differed from that of the undoped CDs, with relatively shorter fluorescence observed for the undoped CDs than for the N,S-CDs. This property signified the different fluorescent species according to the doping of N and S atoms into the CDs.

The chiroptical phenomenon of the N,S-CDs and the undoped counterpart was investigated using circular dichroism spectroscopy, as displayed in Fig. S4 (ESM). Interestingly, the circular dichroism spectra of the undoped CDs were unable to be recorded, whereas that of the N,S-CDs showed a strong trisignate circular dichroism pattern with positive Cotton effects at 215 and 350 nm and a negative Cotton effect at 315 nm. Remarkably, the circular dichroism feature of the N,S-CDs was clearly distinguished from the circular dichroism spectra of l -cysteine and the mixture of CDs + L-cysteine. The optical activity characteristic of the prepared codoped CDs may be derived from their chiral core or the chirality of their corona [30]. These results supported the results from other characterization techniques in which CDs were doped with l -cysteine, not simply a physical mixture of the two components.

As a fluorescent probe, the codoped CDs were evaluated for their response toward the detection of Mn2+ (Fig. 3 ). As displayed in Fig. 3(a), the N,S-CDs possessed a weak fluorescent emission characteristic peak at 510 nm when excitation occurred at 434 nm. However, upon the addition of a few micromoles of Mn2+, a significant increase in the N,S-CDs fluorescence intensity was observed with a slight redshift in the spectrum. The systematic study was evaluated in parallel using undoped CDs synthesized from lactose under exactly the same conditions as the N,S-CDs preparation but without l -cysteine. The results in Fig. 3(b) clearly show no significant difference in the fluorescence intensity of the undoped CDs when Mn2+ was added. Furthermore, the response of the mixture of the undoped CDs with l -cysteine to Mn2+ was studied. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the presence of l -cysteine in the solution did not enable the CDs to become sensitive to Mn2+. Based on this experiment, the N,S-CDs exhibited strong potential as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Mn2+ through a currently unexplored fluorescence enhancement strategy. In addition, this result confirmed the role of doping l -cysteine into the structure of the CDs (from the pyrolysis of lactose) in the detection of Mn2+, and the unreacted l -cysteine did not interfere with the detection of this ion.

A possible sensing mechanism involving the increase in fluorescence of the N,S-CDs following the addition of Mn2+ was elucidated using several techniques. Upon adding Mn2+, the fluorescence was increased, and the redshifted spectrum suggested that the reaction occurred during the Mn2+ addition and that the structure of the as-prepared codoped CDs was improved to become a more efficient fluorophore. This result corresponded with the absorption spectrum of the N,S-CDs, as shown in Fig. 3(d). Their absorption spectrum changed after the reaction with Mn2+. The absorption shoulder peak at approximately 425 nm increased, while the absorption peak at approximately 300 nm decreased. This result suggested an increase in the π-π* or n-π* transition associated with carbon nanomaterials [27] that was responsible for the observed fluorescence enhancement. In addition, compared with the absorption spectrum of the parallel analogous systems (Fig. 3(e) and (f)), the CDs exhibited no change in the absorption spectrum upon the addition of Mn2+. Thus, Mn2+ may react with the doped part of the carbon-based structure. The effect of the addition of excess l -cysteine was determined to confirm this hypothesis, and the results are shown in Fig. S5 (ESM). Adding excess l -cysteine to the detection system did not increase the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs. In fact, the presence of excess l -cysteine in the system inhibited the fluorescence enhancement due to its reducing ability, suggesting that the increase in fluorescence was not caused by the reaction between excess l -cysteine and Mn2+.

Notably, the codoped CDs were synthesized in NaOH medium. Basically, Mn2+ formed the hydroxide complex Mn(OH)2 and was readily oxidized by dissolved oxygen in water to yield MnO2. MnO2 is a moderate oxidizing agent that potentially oxidizes reducing compounds. We hypothesized that upon the addition of Mn2+ to NaOH in the N,S-CDs solution, MnO2 formed and oxidized an incompletely doped part of the CDs to yield the N,S-doped graphitic carbon structure, resulting in increased fluorescence. We investigated the effect of dissolved O2 in the reaction medium on fluorescence by saturating the solution containing the N,S-CDs with N2 gas before the addition of Mn2+ and investigating the change in fluorescence compared with the system without N2 saturation. The results are shown in Fig. S6 (ESM). As expected, the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs in the N2-saturated solution in the presence of Mn2+ was increased by a lower magnitude than that of the system without N2 saturation. This result revealed that O2 in the solution medium affected the formation of MnO2. A lower concentration of dissolved oxygen provided a lower degree of fluorescence enhancement. In addition, the conversion of Mn2+ into MnO2 by dissolved oxygen was confirmed. Furthermore, the effect of ascorbic acid was studied to prove that the increase in fluorescence relied on the oxidizing ability of MnO2. According to the results shown in Fig. S7 (ESM), ascorbic acid did not alter the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs, which was enhanced in the presence of Mn2+. In the system containing ascorbic acid, the N,S-CDs did not display an increase in fluorescence intensity upon the addition of Mn2+. This lack of change was due to ascorbic acid, which is known as a good reducing agent, destroying MnO2 (oxidizing agent), which in turn prohibited the increase in the fluorescence intensity of the codoped CDs. This result clearly suggested that the fluorescence enhancement observed upon the addition of Mn2+ resulted from the oxidation of N,S-CDs by MnO2.

Next, we elucidated the sensing mechanism using the Mn edge X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) technique to investigate the change in the oxidation state of Mn during detection. Based on the result presented in Fig. 4 (a), the pre-edge position of Mn2+ in NaOH solution (MnO2) at 6548.4 eV matched the standard MnO2 solid at 6551.7 eV [31,32], confirming the predominance of a Mn(IV) oxidation state when mixing Mn2+ with the NaOH solution.

In addition, the pre-edge position at 6548 eV (Fig. 4(b)) was observed upon Mn2+ addition to the N,S-CDs solution. This result confirmed that Mn2+ was also converted to MnO2 (mixed with unreacted Mn2+) when mixed with the synthesized N,S-CDs solution. Moreover, Fig. 4(c) shows that the pre-edge position was shifted from 6548 eV to 6544.7 eV when the amount of the N,S-CDs solution was increased, which clearly confirmed that MnO2 was partially converted to Mn2+ after the reaction with the N,S-CDs [37]. The XANES results strongly confirmed the oxidation of the codoped CDs by MnO2, which was generated upon adding Mn2+ and resulted in a fluorescence enhancement.

FTIR spectra of the N,S-CDs were measured in the presence and absence of added Mn2+ to observe the change in the composition of the functional groups of codoped CDs after reacting with Mn2+. The associated FTIR spectra are shown in Fig. S8 (ESM). The main functional groups that were characteristic of the N,S-CDs were still observed after reacting with Mn2+ but showed a small shift to longer wavenumbers. These results may be attributed to the change in the structural orientation or the different environmental structures as a result of the reaction with MnO2.

The size and morphology of the N,S-CDs particles before and after adding Mn2+ were also evaluated using HR-TEM, as shown in Fig. 1(c) and (e) and Fig. 1 (e) and (f), respectively. Neither the morphology nor particle size of the codoped CDs was altered after the addition of Mn2+, and particle aggregation was not observed. However, as shown in Fig. 1(f), the codoped CDs had new lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of approximately 0.31 nm after the reaction with MnO2, suggesting a change in the crystal structure of the graphitic carbon structure.

Furthermore, the fluorescent lifetime of the N,S-CDs at 430 nm was investigated to explore the relaxation behavior of the excited electron of the N,S-CDs during the detection of Mn2+. The fluorescence decay profile and its fitting parameters are shown in Fig. S9 (ESM). The fluorescent decay profile of the codoped CDs in the absence of Mn2+ was significantly different from that of the system with Mn2+. The fluorescent lifetime of the N,S-CDs after reaction with Mn2+ was relatively longer than that of the N,S-CDs alone. After the addition of Mn2+, fluorescence was emitted from the modified carbon structure (as a result of the reaction with MnO2), which induced the radioactive recombination of excitations. All of the evidence obtained using various techniques supported this hypothesis as well. Therefore, we concluded that the Mn2+ in a highly alkaline solution was converted to MnO2, after which the N,S-CDs were oxidized by the generated MnO2, resulting in a fluorescence enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameters affecting detection sensitivity and their optimization
Types of buffers and solution pH
ce:section.ce:para: We explored the effects of various experimental conditions, such as the solution pH, incubation time, concentration of N,S-CDs and incubation time, to obtain the highest detection sensitivity. The pH of the solution was the first parameter that was studied for the detection of aqueous solutions. For the proposed assay, the solution pH might be altered at two steps: the incubation step and the detection step. For the incubation step, the solution pH must be maintained with NaOH, a strong alkali medium, to convert Mn2+ to MnO2. The solution pH of this step, therefore, was not subjected to detailed optimization but remained the same as the synthesis condition. The solution pH must be maintained at a constant value with a suitable buffer after the incubation of Mn2+ in the medium of the codoped CDs to control the pH during detection. The experiments were performed by adjusting the pH of the mixture consisting of N,S-CDs and Mn2+ with 0.1 M ACBF to achieve pH 4–6, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to achieve pH 6.5–7.0 and Tris-HCl to achieve pH 7.5–9.5. The effect of the solution pH on the detection of Mn2+ by N,S-CDs is shown in Fig. 5 . The type of buffer and the solution pH had insignificant effects on the fluorescent emission of the N,S-CDs. In contrast, the type of buffer and solution pH altered the extent to which the fluorescence of the N,S-CDs increased after the reaction with MnO2. The highest degree of fluorescence enhancement for the detection of Mn2+ by the N,S-CDs was achieved using 0.1 M ACBF at pH 5.0. Thus, the mixture of N,S-CDs and Mn2+ was adjusted to pH 5.0 with 0.1 M ACBF before recording the fluorescence spectrum.

The incubation time after mixing N,S-CDs and Mn2+ may affect the detection sensitivity because Mn2+ must be first converted to MnO2 and oxidize the N,S-CDs. Thus, the effect of the incubation time on the efficiency of increasing the fluorescence of the N,S-CDs by Mn2+ was studied. The experiment was conducted by mixing N,S-CDs and MnO2 and incubating them for 0–60 min followed by a pH adjustment of the solution with 0.1 M ACBF prior to the fluorescence measurement. The fluorescence spectra obtained after mixing with Mn2+ and the fluorescence intensity as a function of incubation time are displayed in Fig. 6 (a) and (b). Based on the fluorescence spectra, the reaction between the N,S-CDs and Mn2+ occurred immediately after mixing, and fluorescent emission was immediately be recorded without incubation. This result suggests that the proposed assay was very fast and able to measure signals after less than 5 min. Although the reaction occurred very fast, for the best precision, the mixture was incubated for 5 min prior to adjusting the solution pH. In addition, the effect of the incubation time after adjusting the solution pH with 0.1 M ACBF, pH 5, was also studied. The results in Fig. 6 (c) and (d) show the stability of the fluorescence spectrum obtained 5 min after adjusting the solution pH. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the detection system in the buffer solution was very stable for at least 1 h. This characteristic also provides a high-precision method. Therefore, the mixture was incubated for 5 min prior to measuring fluorescence, and the time of 5 min was subsequently used in further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the N,S-CDs volume on the Mn2+ detection sensitivity
ce:section.ce:para: Basically, the amount of the probe may exert a direct effect on the detection sensitivity and working concentration range. The amount of the synthesized material used in the proposed assay was therefore investigated. This experiment was performed by varying the volume of the N,S-CDs used in the assay from 50 to 200 μL in the presence of 5 μM Mn2+. The pH of the mixture was adjusted to 5.0 with 0.1 M ACBF followed by a 5 min incubation prior to measuring the fluorescence. The fluorescence spectra recorded after adding Mn2+ to different volumes of the N,S-CDs are depicted in Fig. 7 (a), and their corresponding fluorescence intensities as a function of the N,S-CDs volume are shown in Fig. 7(b). In the absence of Mn2+, the fluorescence intensities of the N,S-CDs increased when the volume of the N,S-CDs increased due to the increasing number of N,S-CDs in the solution. Upon the addition of Mn2+, the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs increased when the volume of the N,S-CDs was increased up to 100 μL, after which point it remained constant due to the limited concentration of Mn2+. This result clearly revealed the substantial effect of the availability of the N,S-CDs on the sensitivity and the working range of the probe. N,S-CDs were added at a volume of 100 μL when evaluating other parameters to ensure that the number of N,S-CDs exceeded that of Mn2+ in the detection system and to ensure the best detection sensitivity coupled with a wide linear range of working concentrations.

One of the most important parameters to determine the success of newly developed sensors is their selectivity. The response to other related species may bias the accuracy of the detection. In the present study, the target species was Mn2+; thus, the behavior of N,S-CDs toward other cations must be carefully evaluated. The fluorescence spectra of the codoped CDs in the presence of other metal ions at a concentration of 5 μM were recorded under the optimized detection conditions. The results in Fig. 8 show that the fluorescence spectrum of the N,S-CDs was not significantly altered by other metal ions compared with the response obtained in the presence of Mn2+. Thus, the N,S-CDs were much more selective to the detection of Mn2+ than other cations. The selectivity of this sensor in the detection of Mn2+ may arise from the specific reaction of the generated MnO2 with the as-prepared N,S-CDs. In addition, most of the metal ions participated in metal hydroxide complexes in highly alkaline media. Furthermore, because the change in signal was based on the fluorescence enhancement strategy instead of fluorescence quenching, relatively few possible interfering species were available. The finding that the developed sensor achieved good selectivity toward Mn2+ clearly indicated its promising potential in real-world applications for Mn2+ detection in environmental samples.

In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs was not affected by Sn2+. This result signified that the N,S-CDs possess good selectivity for the detection of Mn2+. Moreover, we also investigated the effect of Sn2+ on interfering with the detection of Mn2+. Basically, Sn2+ is a reducing cation that may scavenge the generated MnO2, which is the oxidizing species. The result in Fig. S10 clearly shows that the degree of fluorescence enhancement (F–F0) in the presence of Mn2+ alone did not significantly differ from the system that also contained Sn2+. This result signified that the generated MnO2 was not reduced by Sn2+ because the conditions are not suitable for MnO2 reduction by Sn2+. Therefore, Sn2+ did not interfere with the detection of Mn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the proposed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed probe was validated using the optimized parameters to determine its analytical performance. For this experiment, the fluorescence spectrum of the codoped CDs was measured in the presence of increasing Mn2+ concentrations. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), the fluorescence spectral intensity exhibited a gradual increase with increasing Mn2+ concentration, along with a small redshift in the spectra. The level of fluorescence enhancement (F–F0) at 510 nm was plotted against the Mn2+ concentration to construct the calibration graph shown in Fig. 9(b). An excellent linear relationship showing an increasing trend between the fluorescence and the Mn2+ concentration was observed in the range of 0–5 μM with the regression equation F–F0 = 26.38([Mn2+], μM] + 1.30 (r2 = 0.9986). The degree of fluorescence enhancement (F–F0) when the concentration of Mn2+ was greater than 5 μM deviated from the linear relationship, and the degree of fluorescence enhancement decreased (Fig. S11). This decrease may be due to the limited amount of N,S-CDs in the assay, which was sufficient for the detection of Mn2+ concentrations less than 5 μM. The fluorescence quenching when the concentration of Mn2+ was greater than 5 μM arises from the inner filter effect (IFE) phenomenon. As shown in Fig. S12, the absorption spectrum of MnO2 overlapped with the excitation spectrum of N,S-CDs, which caused IFE and resulted in fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the working range of the sensor was limited to 0–5 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was evaluated by recording the fluorescence signals of the N,S-CDs at 510 nm in the absence of Mn2+ (blank solution) for 10 replicates, and the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity was then computed. The LOD and limit of quantitation (LOQ) were evaluated based on the concentration of Mn2+ that provided a signal 3 times and 10 times greater than the standard deviation of the blank solution, respectively. The LOD was 0.12 μM, whereas the LOQ was 0.52 μM. These results revealed that the developed sensor possessed high sensitivity and was useful to detect very low trace concentrations of Mn2+.

A few fluorescence sensors for the detection of Mn2+ have been developed. The important performance characteristics of each reported sensor are summarized in Table S3. The reported fluorescence probes were derived from various types of nanomaterials [38–40] and exhibited an LOD at the micromolar level with a wide working linear concentration range. Remarkably, the LOD in the present study is significantly improved compared with previous reports. However, the analytical performance characteristic of N,S-CDs in terms of the working concentration range may not be improved compared with other reported probes. However, the sensitivity, selectivity, and simplicity of the preparation of N,S-CDs are quite remarkable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Mn2+ concentrations in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor was applied to assess Mn2+ concentrations in real water samples and determine its feasibility in real applications. Two sample types of tap water and wastewater were selected as representative environmental samples. The water samples and their spiked samples were analyzed with the proposed sensor under the optimal conditions. These two sample groups were also analyzed using the standard ICP–OES technique to evaluate the accuracy of our developed method. The results in Table 1 show that Mn2+ was not detected in tap water or in wastewater. However, the sensor detected Mn2+ in spiked samples with excellent recoveries in the range of 99–101%, and the %RSDs were less than 3%. In addition, the results obtained from the proposed assay were consistent with those obtained from the standard ICP–OES technique and signified that the proposed sensor possessed good accuracy but had a lower analytical cost than ICP–OES. Based on the analysis of the water samples, the potential of the proposed sensor was confirmed, paving the way for its real-world applications with good accuracy and precision.

In conclusion, we successfully developed an elegant approach based on the use of N,S-doped CDs to selectively detect Mn2+. The codoped CDs were synthesized using a very simple, one-pot method involving the pyrolysis of lactose with l -cysteine in a hot NaOH solution. The material was characterized using several techniques, confirming the successful synthesis of the proposed sensor. Interestingly, the presence of Mn2+ effectively increased the fluorescence signals of the N,S-CDs. The sensing mechanism was based on the oxidation of the N,S-CDs by MnO2, which was generated upon the addition of Mn2+ into the strong alkaline medium of the N,S-CDs. The codoped CDs exhibited excellent selectivity toward Mn2+ over other cations. Application of the sensor for the determination of the Mn2+ content in contaminated water yielded good accuracy and precision, and the obtained results corresponded well with those obtained using the standard ICP–OES technique. All the results indicated the great applicability of the N,S-CDs for the detection of Mn2+ in other applications.

Wittaya Ngeontae: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing, and Funding Acquisition, Kanokwan Chaiendoo: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, and Visualization, Kessarin Ngamdee: Supervision, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, and Writing - Review & Editing, Sukhum Ruangchai: Writing - Review & Editing, Chatree Saiyasombat: Supervision and Investigation, Wutthikrai Busayaporn: Supervision and Investigation, Somlak Ittisanronnachai: Investigation and Methodology, Vinich Promarak: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT) contract number NRCT5-RSA63003-04. WN thanks the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. This work was carried out under the Network on Emerging Sensor Designs and Fabrication Strategies, the Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI) (IRN62W0002).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiple scattering calculations of bonding and x-ray absorption spectroscopy of manganese oxides

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gilbert B, Frazer BH, Belz A, Conrad PG, Nealson K, Haskel HD, Lang JC, Srajer G, De Stasio G. Multiple scattering calculations of bonding and x-ray absorption spectroscopy of manganese oxides. J. Phys. Chem. A 2003;107:2839-2847. https://doi.org/10.1021/jp021493s.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A pre-edge analysis of Mn K-edge XANES spectra to help determine the speciation of manganese in minerals and glasses

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chalmin E., Farges F., Brown G.E. Jr. A pre-edge analysis of Mn K-edge XANES spectra to help determine the speciation of manganese in minerals and glasses. Contrib Mineral Petrol 2009;157:111–126. doi:10.1007/s00410-008-0323-z.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silicon nanoparticles: fluorescent, colorimetric and gel membrane multiple detection of Cu2+ and Mn2+ as well rapid visualization of latent fingerprints

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu B., Tang M., Yu L., Qu Y., Chai F., Chen L., et al. Silicon nanoparticles: fluorescent, colorimetric and gel membrane multiple detection of Cu2+ and Mn2+ as well rapid visualization of latent fingerprints. Anal Methods 2019;11:3570–3577. doi:10.1039/c9ay01011d.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An “on–off–on” fluorescence assay based on silicon nanoparticles for selective detection of manganese(II)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Meng L., Yuan Y, Pu G., Xu N. An “on–off–on” fluorescence assay based on silicon nanoparticles for selective detection of manganese(II). Anal Methods 2017;9:2553–2560. doi:10.1039/c7ay00514h.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent copper nanoparticles for label-free detection of manganese ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ma S.Y., Yeh Y.C. One-step synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent copper nanoparticles for label-free detection of manganese ions. Anal Methods 2015;7:6475–6478. doi:10.1039/c5ay01567g.
